description: Good/bad post training examples for edtech, STEM education, and chemistry education social media content.

qualityKey.green: Substantive — has a point of view, teaches something, invites thought, shows real expertise or passion. Ends with a brief open-ended discussion prompt.
qualityKey.red: Low-quality — vague praise, generic cheerleading, no substance, no perspective.

examples.text: This is so cool! I really value what this team is doing.
High quality STEM curricula is so important!
Chemistry is amazing! Everyone should learn it. 🔬
Love seeing more STEM content out there. Keep it up!
We need more people talking about science education. So important!
This is exactly what the next generation needs. Great work!
Edtech is changing the world. Excited to see where it goes!
As a chemistry lover, I approve this message. 👏
STEM education is the future. Period.
More of this please! Science teachers deserve all the support.
It's painful to watch a teacher take away a student's chance to struggle productively. What's a concept you had to wrestle with before it finally clicked?

I'm a big believer in low-structure, project-based learning. Sparking a real interest in chemistry matters far more to me than memorizing facts, and I wish our academic incentives reflected that. What would you change first — the grading or the curriculum?

Comedians are engaging because they tell stories through fun characters. You can make a question more engaging the same way — by framing it as a story told by a playful character. My favorites are the ones narrated by a chemist. Who's a character or persona that made a STEM topic stick for you?

The moment a student realizes that the rust on their bike chain is the same reaction they just balanced on paper — that's when chemistry stops being a class and starts being a lens. What everyday object made a concept click for you?

Most chemistry curricula front-load vocabulary and back-load wonder. I'd rather hand a student a rusting nail and a diamond ring on day one and let them wrestle with why carbon does such different things. If you could teach only one concept on day one, what would it be?

Unpopular take: flashy lab demos can actually hurt learning if the student walks away remembering the explosion but not the exothermic mechanism behind it. A honey jar teaches viscosity better than any slow-motion video. What's a 'boring' object that taught you more than a flashy demo ever did?

I tutor Gen Z chemistry students and the ones who retain the most are never the ones who memorized the periodic table first. They're the ones who argued with me about whether a soap bubble is really a colloid. When's the last time a student's pushback actually taught you something?

Edtech keeps building tools that help students answer questions faster. I'm more interested in tools that help students sit with a question longer. What's a tool or technique you use to slow students down in a good way?

One thing I've noticed teaching AP Chem: students can parrot 'entropy is disorder' but freeze when you ask them why their earbuds tangle in their pocket. What's your go-to real-world example for a concept students always get wrong?

The best chemistry teachers I've watched don't simplify — they anchor. They pick one concrete object, like a cast iron skillet for specific heat, and build the entire abstraction around it. What's your anchor object?

Hot take for edtech founders: stop building platforms that replace the teacher. Build ones that give a first-year teacher the same instinct a 20-year veteran has — the instinct to hold up a sealed mason jar when a student asks where the mass goes. What instinct took you years to develop that you wish you'd had on day one?

I keep a running list of everyday objects that map to chemistry concepts. A bike pump for Boyle's law. A sugar cube for solubility. A DNA swab for nucleic acids. What's on your list that isn't on mine?

We talk a lot about making STEM 'accessible' but rarely about making it sticky. Accessibility gets a student through the door. Stickiness is what happens when they see a lemon at the grocery store and involuntarily think about pH. What concept lives rent-free in your head because of how you first learned it?

The gap between a student who passes chemistry and a student who thinks like a chemist is almost always one good analogy. Not a simulation, not a video — just one physical object that made an invisible idea real enough to argue about. What analogy changed how you see a concept?

If your chemistry visual aid requires a paragraph of explanation before the student gets it, it's not a visual aid — it's another thing to memorize. The best ones work because the student already knows what a melting ice cream cone looks like. You're just giving it a name: second law of thermodynamics. What's the most self-explanatory visual you've ever used?
